items.title: Hash passwords with PBKDF2, per-user salt and a fixed-time compare
items.description: Store every password as a salted, iterated key derivative rather than the password itself. Derive the key with PBKDF2 over a strong PRF (for example HMAC-SHA-256) using a fresh cryptographically random salt per user and a high iteration count (on the order of 100,000). Persist the algorithm parameters alongside the key in a single self-describing field such as `{iterations}.{salt}.{key}` with each part base64-encoded, so verification can read back the exact parameters used at hashing time.
items.applicability: The credential-storage primitive behind a real login: a PasswordHasher component that exposes Hash(password) and Verify(password, stored) and is the only place credentials are turned into or checked against stored material.
items.example: PasswordHasher.Hash("Admin#12345") generates 16 random salt bytes, runs PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 for 100,000 iterations to a 32-byte key, and returns "100000.<base64 salt>.<base64 key>". Verify splits the stored value on '.', re-derives the key with the same salt and iteration count, and compares.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compare the derived key with a constant-time routine such as CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals so verification time does not leak how many leading bytes matched; never use ordinary string or array equality. Test implication: assert a hash round-trips (Hash then Verify is true) and that a wrong password returns false. Honest boundary: PBKDF2 is a sound, widely available choice but is not memory-hard; threat models that must resist large GPU/ASIC cracking farms should consider memory-hard derivations (scrypt, Argon2) instead, and the iteration count must be revisited over time as hardware improves.

items.title: Never store plaintext or reversible passwords
items.description: A credential store must keep only one-way derivatives of passwords. Plaintext columns, two-way encryption of passwords, unsalted single-round hashes, and password values written to logs or audit text are all prohibited. The AppUser entity carries a single PasswordHash column whose contents cannot be reversed to recover the password, and the raw password exists only transiently in memory during hashing or verification.
items.applicability: The schema and data-access rules for the user table, plus any seeding, import, or admin-reset path that sets a credential.
items.example: AppUser has a PasswordHash string column and no Password column. A seeding routine sets PasswordHash = PasswordHasher.Hash(seedPassword); the seed password literal appears only in the seeder source, never in the database, logs, or audit rows.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: forbid logging or echoing the raw password anywhere, including exception messages and audit event payloads, and ensure the hash field is excluded from any generic entity-to-log serialization. Test implication: assert the persisted PasswordHash never equals the raw password and that it parses as the expected `{iterations}.{salt}.{key}` shape. Honest boundary: this protects passwords at rest in the database, but does not protect a password observed in transit or in a memory dump; transport security and process hardening are separate concerns.

items.title: Use a named cookie authentication scheme with explicit sign-in claims
items.description: Register a cookie-based authentication scheme with a stable scheme name and wire the request pipeline to authenticate then authorize. On a successful credential check, build a ClaimsIdentity that carries at least a Name claim for the username and one Role claim per resolved role, wrap it in a ClaimsPrincipal, and issue the auth cookie via the framework sign-in API. Subsequent requests are identified by decrypting that cookie back into the same principal.
items.applicability: The web host wiring (Program.cs / startup) and the controller action that completes a login by signing the user in.
items.example: AddAuthentication with the default scheme "LafErpCookie" plus AddCookie configuring the login and access-denied paths; the pipeline calls UseAuthentication() before UseAuthorization(). On login the action creates a ClaimsIdentity with a Name claim and Role claims, then signs in with SignInAsync.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: UseAuthentication must come before UseAuthorization in the pipeline or principals will be empty when authorization runs; keep the scheme name a single shared constant so sign-in and challenge agree. Test implication: a Playwright login flow should land on an authenticated page and a protected route should redirect an anonymous request to the login path. Honest boundary: a cookie scheme covers interactive browser sessions for a single app; it is not a token scheme for APIs or cross-service single sign-on, which require their own design.

items.title: Seed an initial administrator with a pre-hashed password
items.description: A fresh deployment needs at least one usable account, so the data seeder must create an initial administrator (and any demonstration users) by running the chosen password through the same PasswordHasher used at login, then writing the resulting hash plus the linking role rows. Seeding never inserts a plaintext or specially-formatted credential that bypasses the normal verification path.
items.applicability: The DataSeeder routine that runs on first startup or schema creation to make the system immediately loginable.
items.example: The seeder creates admin (System Manager + Accounts Manager), alice (Sales User), and bob (Accounts Manager + Accounts User), each with PasswordHash = PasswordHasher.Hash(<seed password>) and the matching AppUserRole rows, so admin/Admin#12345 logs in through the ordinary Authenticate path.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make seeding idempotent so re-running does not duplicate users or roles, and gate it so it only runs against an empty or freshly created store. Test implication: a test should authenticate the seeded admin and confirm the expected roles are resolved. Honest boundary: seeded credentials are well-known demonstration defaults and must be changed or disabled before any real exposure; the seeder is a convenience, not a production credential-management policy.

items.title: Map user to roles to role claims for authorization
items.description: Model authorization as a join: a user has many roles through a link table, and each role has a name. At authentication time, load the active user including its roles, resolve the role names, and emit one Role claim per name into the principal so that role-based authorization attributes and policy checks can read them. Authorization decisions are made against the role claims on the principal, not against ad-hoc user flags.
items.applicability: The Authenticate path that returns the resolved role set, and the controller/action authorization annotations that gate features by role.
items.example: AppUser -> AppUserRole -> AppRole. Authenticate loads the user with its AppUserRole rows, projects role names like "Accounts Manager", and the login action adds a Role claim for each; an action annotated to require "Accounts Manager" then admits bob and admin but not alice.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: include the role navigation in the user query (or project the names) so the role set is fully materialized before claims are built; a lazy or missing include yields a logged-in user with no roles. Test implication: assert the seeded admin authenticates and the returned role set contains the expected names. Honest boundary: this is coarse role-based access control; fine-grained per-record or per-field permissions, hierarchical roles, and delegation are not implied and would need an explicit permission model.

items.title: Record a Login audit event on successful authentication
items.description: A successful authentication should leave an audit trail. The authentication service writes a "Login" audit event identifying the user and the time as part of the same unit of work that confirms the credential, then commits. This produces a durable, queryable record of who signed in and when, separate from web-server access logs.
items.applicability: The UserAuthService.Authenticate path, immediately after a verified credential and before returning the success result.
items.example: Authenticate verifies the hash, resolves roles, appends a Login audit event for the username, and calls SaveChanges once so the verification outcome and the audit entry persist together.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: write the audit row in the same SaveChanges as the rest of the login bookkeeping so a partial commit cannot leave a login without its audit (or an audit without effect); never put the password into the audit payload. Test implication: assert that a successful authenticate produces exactly one Login audit row for the user. Honest boundary: this records only the success case described here; failed-login auditing, lockout counters, and tamper-evident or append-only audit storage are separate features that must be added deliberately.

items.title: Resolve the current user from the authenticated principal with a guarded dev fallback
items.description: A current-user accessor should prefer the authenticated principal: when the request carries a signed-in identity, the username and roles come from its claims. Only when there is no authenticated principal may it fall back to a development cookie or default identity, so local and deterministic test runs still resolve a stable user without a full login. The real principal always wins when present.
items.applicability: An HttpCurrentUser / ICurrentUser abstraction consumed by services and controllers that need to know who is acting.
items.example: HttpCurrentUser checks HttpContext.User.Identity.IsAuthenticated first and returns its Name and Role claims; if not authenticated it reads a dev cookie to supply a fallback username so headless tests and local browsing have a deterministic actor.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: order the resolution so the authenticated principal is checked before the fallback, and confine the fallback to development by configuration so it cannot silently grant identity in a real deployment. Test implication: assert that an authenticated principal overrides the dev fallback and that the fallback yields a deterministic user when no principal is present. Honest boundary: the dev fallback is a testability affordance, not a security control; if it is reachable in production it becomes an authentication bypass, so it must be gated off there.

items.title: Cover authentication with hash round-trip, wrong-password, seeded-login and audit assertions
items.description: Authentication earns trust through a small, pointed test set: (1) a password hash round-trips so Hash then Verify is true; (2) a wrong password is rejected by Verify; (3) the seeded administrator authenticates through the real service and the expected roles are resolved; (4) a successful authentication writes the Login audit event. These unit tests pin the security-critical behaviour independently of the UI.
items.applicability: The xUnit (or equivalent) suite for the auth layer, run as part of the standard build gate.
items.example: Four tests: PasswordHasher round-trip true; Verify(wrongPassword) false; UserAuthService.Authenticate("admin","Admin#12345") returns Ok with the seeded roles; the same call adds one Login audit row.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive these tests through the same PasswordHasher and Authenticate methods the application uses, against an isolated in-memory or throwaway store, so they exercise real code rather than stubs. Test implication: keep the wrong-password and round-trip cases as explicit, separately named tests so a regression points to the exact broken invariant. Honest boundary: these four cases prove the core happy path and the most important negative case; they do not by themselves cover lockout, concurrency, timing-attack resistance, or every malformed-input path.

items.title: Give login UI stable data-testid hooks for end-to-end tests
items.description: The login view should expose stable, semantic test identifiers on the elements an end-to-end test must drive: the form, the username and password inputs, the submit control, and the error region. These hooks let a browser-automation suite locate elements by intent rather than by brittle CSS or text, so the test survives styling and copy changes.
items.applicability: The Login.cshtml (or equivalent) markup and the Playwright/browser tests that perform a real sign-in.
items.example: Login.cshtml tags the form, username, password, submit button, and error block with data-testid values login-form, username, password, login-submit, and login-error; two Playwright tests use these to assert a good login succeeds and a bad login surfaces login-error.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: treat the data-testid values as a stable contract shared with the tests, changed deliberately and in lockstep with them. Test implication: select elements by data-testid rather than by visible text or layout so theming and localization do not break the suite. Honest boundary: stable selectors make end-to-end tests reliable but do not by themselves assert security; the assertions still have to check the right outcomes (authenticated landing, visible error) rather than just element presence.

items.title: Define explicit login and access-denied redirect paths
items.description: The cookie scheme must declare where unauthenticated and unauthorized requests go. An anonymous request to a protected resource is challenged and redirected to the login path, carrying a return-url so the user resumes their original destination after signing in; an authenticated-but-unauthorized request is sent to a distinct access-denied path. Logout signs the principal out and clears the cookie.
items.applicability: The cookie options configuration plus the AccountController GET/POST Login and Logout actions.
items.example: Cookie options set LoginPath to the Account login and AccessDeniedPath to a forbidden page; a logged-out visit to a guarded page redirects to login with a returnUrl, and Logout calls SignOutAsync then redirects home.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate or constrain the return-url to local paths before redirecting back to it so the login flow cannot be turned into an open redirect. Test implication: assert an anonymous protected-route request redirects to the login path and that logout clears the authenticated state. Honest boundary: redirect wiring controls navigation, not entitlement; the protected actions themselves must still enforce the required roles, since a redirect alone does not authorize anything.

items.title: Make hash parameters upgradable so iterations can be rotated
items.description: Because the stored hash records its own iteration count and salt, the system can raise the work factor over time without invalidating existing credentials. Verification reads the parameters embedded in the stored value, so old hashes keep verifying; on a successful login the service can transparently re-hash the supplied password at the current, higher iteration count and persist the upgraded value. New hashes always use the current default.
items.applicability: The PasswordHasher format and an optional re-hash-on-login step in the authentication service.
items.example: A user whose stored hash begins "50000." logs in successfully; because the current default is 100000, the service re-hashes the verified password to "100000.<salt>.<key>" and saves it, so the next login verifies against the stronger parameters.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive verification entirely from the parameters stored in the value (never a hard-coded iteration constant) so mixed-vintage hashes coexist; perform any re-hash inside the same successful-login unit of work. Test implication: verify that a hash produced with a lower iteration count still verifies and, if re-hash is enabled, that the stored value is upgraded. Honest boundary: rehash-on-login only upgrades accounts that actually log in; dormant accounts keep their old parameters until they next authenticate, so rotation is gradual rather than immediate.

items.title: Treat this as application-level auth and state its boundaries honestly
items.description: The authentication described here is a self-contained, application-level scheme: salted iterated password hashing, a cookie session, role claims, seeded users, and login auditing. It deliberately does not include multi-factor authentication, external single sign-on or federation, account lockout and throttling, password-complexity and rotation policies, email-based reset, or breach detection. Those are real requirements for many deployments and must be added as explicit features, not assumed.
items.applicability: Scoping, security review, and any claim made about what the generated auth layer does and does not guarantee.
items.example: A reviewer asks whether the system resists credential-stuffing; the honest answer is that it has no lockout or rate limiting yet, so that protection would need to be added before exposure to that threat.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: document the absent controls so operators know what to add before exposing the system; do not let demonstration defaults imply production readiness. Test implication: tests assert the implemented behaviour (hashing, sign-in, roles, audit) and must not be read as evidence for unimplemented protections such as MFA or lockout. Honest boundary: this item is itself a boundary statement — its value is in preventing overclaiming, and it must be updated whenever one of the listed controls is genuinely added.
